item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Giraffe child parent replication system. Intriguing and strange in all the right ways a skip should be.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How about third generation Stage 3s from different lines?

How about third generation Stage 1s, even?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a fair point about the baby D-class.
Because D-class status is for humans, and the babies would be considered merely humanoid products of an anomaly.

Looking back on it now, offspring should probably get an anomaly designation. Something along the lines of 1169-Beta-<test number>.

I'll give it some more thought and edit if I can see how to tweak without disrupting the parts people like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded on the D class thing. It's why I withheld my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh god! Haha! I remember those things from Conan's skit of celebrity couple babies. Those were hideous! That would be a pretty freaky effect!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems like double exposure should be incapable of expressing unexpressed traits from either parent,and should still express accrued traits from both. Perhaps rather than determining (for example) eye color normally, it would blend the colorations of the parents. With other features this might yield some uncanny-valley results - nose shapes and such that don't quite occur normally. Its like those bad photoshop-to-see-your-children effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2 things. I was pretty put off that the daughter of the D-class in the last addendum was also labeled D-class. Regardless of headcanons and whether or not monthly terminations happen, it just seems off to me that the Foundation would have a child born into D-class status.

Second, while I realize this is waaaay late in the game and unnecessary to include in the article at this point, for double-exposure offspring (meaning the child of two 1169-infected parents), I would simply note that no discernible difference from normal heredity occurs. Since this sorta mimics Lamarckian Evolution, and that theory was created in the face of normally occurring childbirths, the mixing of two "Lamarckian" parents would just appear to be a normal birth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if both parents are exposed to scp-1169 to a similar extent?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand why it's necessary to TERMINATE KILL DESTROY anyone infected by the thing. That and the overly long descriptions of the jar and the fluid make it difficult to enjoy this one

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Of course it had to be a giraffe! Ingenious!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my opinion, this is brilliantly one, especially after reading through the discussion and learning that the intent is that

+ show block
– hide block

The formaldehyde induces Lamarkian Evolution where the offspring evolves to changes in the physical body of the parent instead of by genetics

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This can lead to cases where the offspring is genetically female, but physically and functionally male or vice versa.

I know this is hardly the weirdest thing I've read on the site but
What.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interesting effect on this one, and together with the overall quality of the writing this is a clear upvote for me. Good one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh god this is delicious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The easy way out is "They have fraternal twins".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What about using the horse embryo picture from FaustoV's link and calling it a giraffe? They look very similar except for the length of the neck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A lot of people get to these pages via the "Newly Created" page-I hadn't seen the title at all. =)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Twice is a pattern.
Dihydro-monoxide deleted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've let it stew for a few days, and I just can't think of anything interesting to do with double exposure.
If anyone has any suggestions, feel free to drop me a line. But as it is, I'm going to just leave it alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i would like to know what happen if 2 infected people make a new baby.

P.S: To be honest, i think such a test is unnecesary, and i don't want to see this marvelous thing with a bad [DATA EXPUNGED], because it dooesn't need.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yes, but this item is too weird, it deserves an pic. And if someone can help him to photoshop a pic, i think this will be something epic very evry well

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would take that as a kindness personally. I knew what you meant, but 1) it was jarring because no scientist would call it anything but water in this sort of context, or most contexts, and 2) it made me think you were trying to dress it up and make it sound more important and significant than it was, the way pretentious shampoos will sometimes list "water (aqua)" in the ingredients list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's certainly a contender, but I'm really married to the short neck at this point. It's kind of integral to my concept of the item.

I would really like a picture, because fetal giraffes are just really eerie looking if nothing else, but I think it would have to be something shopped, and shopped well. Since my photoshop skills are poor at best, I'm thinking this will just have to do without a picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I was going for is that the children have normal genetic make-ups, roughly 50% of genes derived from each parent, but nonetheless are physically identical to the exposed parent.

None of the genes are actually expressed, the genes are overridden by the anomaly. It's just that the genes from the exposed parent happen to line up with the child's characteristics. But when a geneticist (who is pre-disposed by training and profession to think that the genes must be doing something) does an analysis, it looks to him like the genes from one parent are completly recessive and the genes from the exposed parent are 100% dominant.

The only way for an 1169 offspring to have Turner Syndrome (in the sense of physical manifestation, they could very well have the genes for it) is if the exposed parent had it. Same for sickle-cell anemia, parkinson's disease, or any other congenital condition.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is a bit of my sense of humor.
In my personal experience, most people don't know enough chemistry and/or don't pay enough attention to realize what is actually being refered to.

If more people find it distracting, I'll probably take it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did mean testes, fixed it. thank you.

If I recall Bio 101 correctly (and that is a slim chance at best), all living things have an equivalent of a circulatory system. In that there is a method for moving water and nutrients into tissues that need it and moving waste products to where they can be eliminated. In plants, the equivalent would be the capalaries running from the roots throughout the plant. Even in single-celled amobea, there are mechanisms to push nutrients to the organelles that need them and push waste by-products out through the membrane.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you, although I'm not sure if I'd agree.

I thought I telegraphed the point pretty hard with the title.

I hadn't though about double-exposures. Hmmm, could make for interesting (albeit messy) testing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was the first piano piece I thought of, no other reason.
Blame too many replays of Resident Evil in my youth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Derp, fixed "sublimation". Which is too bad, I like the way it sounded in my head.

The jar/fetus make the liquid in an anomalous, "bottomless cup" sort of way. The liquid causes the offspring anomalies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nicely done. Congrats.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Note, parent and child are not genetically identical, instead showing the expected distribution of genes from both parents; but genes from the unexposed parent are not expressed. This can lead to cases where the offspring is genetically female, but physically and functionally male or vice versa.

I think that it should just say the the child is phenotypically identical despite being genetically different through anomalous means, since if "genes from the unexposed parent are not expressed" were true then the children of stage-2 exposed women would all have Turner syndrome, and the men would have no children whatsoever since an embryo with one Y chromosome but zero X chromosomes would be nonviable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After reading the explanation of what this is actually doing, I think I like it better. That said, it's just a weird enough collection of effects to be good by itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is great and I like the Lamarckian Evolution part of this, but you may have phrased it a bit too clinical and complicated. For example, was it really needed to say 'dihydro-monoxide' when you could've just said water? Especially how it's not really an unusual impurity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One thing: you describe gonads and ovaries seperately, but to the best of jy knowledge, ovaries are gonads. Did you mean "testes"?

Oh, another thing:

If inhaled or allowed to remain in prolonged contact, SCP-1169-1 will quickly be absorbed into the circulatory system of all plants and animals

The phrasing here is a bit odd considering that plants and a few species of animal don't have circulatory systems (I believe. I don't think that stems allow circulatory passage).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh my god, this IS Lamarkian evolution!

You, sir, are a genius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Formaldehyde proper is in fact a gas; what most people are accustomed to calling "formaldehyde" is a solution of formaldehyde in water. Sublimation is not the correct word for what is being described in the write-up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I know what this is. I had a strange inkling as I read it, but re-reading what was preserved (the stunted-neck Giraffe) clinched it in my head.

This is an SCP that induces Lamarckian Evolution, isn't it? (That is to say, it 'makes' the Lamarckian theory 'correct'.)

… Oh god what happens if two people are exposed in equal ammounts and have a child?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ergh…this is creepy

And I like how you only kept the kinesthetic senses, which makes much more sense than "they know everything the parent knows". Since all it really is is muscle memory of a specific series of movements, it makes sense that it would be passed via this. However, I am not sure why the progeny would have to listen to "Moonlight Sonata" first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree with Mackenzie regarding the description- we get a lot of detail about the fetus and jar that has absolutely no bearing on the anomalous portion (ie, the fluid). Are the jar and fluid anomalous at all?

Also, isn't formaldehyde (spelling, sorry, phone) a liquid? I thought sublimation specifically described the shift from solid to gas.

All of that being said, the effect is novel and interesting. Upvote, with the hope that the execution will get a little more polish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't think this needs a visual aid, really. I think the reader would want to focus more on visualizing the effect than a creepy image of a a giraffe in a jar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. I really like the progression of the effects, and would suggest adding one of the pictures from above. I love stuff like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought about using that pic, but it doesn't quite match the description.
I could tweak the article so that it does match, but I really like some of the details that would get lost. The short-neck on the fetus, for example.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's absolutely no reason for this funky formaldehyde to come from an oversized mason jar holding a giraffe fetus.
That's exactly why it has to.
Also, the stage 3 effects are pure WTF in the best of ways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the glass is too big right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That one fits the description pretty well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Think this would be better, considering it's supposed to from the thirteenth month of gestation:

http://25.media.tumblr.com/tumblr_lny36jYVo21qzd3rjo1_500.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was just about to link that from here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This item is really interesting, original, well explained, bizarre and definitely i don't want to make contact with that or anything similar to this…

+1(00)

Edit: if you find a picture, this scp will be pure gold.
Re edit: check this http://www.google.co.ve/imgres?imgurl=http://4.bp.blogspot.com/_jc1Yb7dlJP4/TA6kyBmrwVI/AAAAAAAAABs/Xw7Aq-VxYYs/s1600/2559430779_2f73746669.jpg&imgrefurl=http://differencesof12days.blogspot.com/&h=375&w=500&sz=36&tbnid=lmnIj6QA4GwbiM:&tbnh=96&tbnw=128&zoom=1&usg=__Ud1RLZhnoAU-QAQKMUkui9ABNTo=&docid=V_MszXKLdPt8TM&hl=es&sa=X&ei=FO3xT7yRHqiv6gGv-fWYBg&ved=0CFgQ9QEwAw&dur=309

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it. Well thought out, well explained, and an interesting little thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Either I'm really tired, I suddenly forgot how to read, or this is just really vague.

What does it do?

What the fuck.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to be honest, I didn't read this entire thing after my eyes started going blurry after the second paragraph. There's way too much runaround before you even start to explain what this thing does, and that's a big turn-off for me.
